speaker: interviewer
text: Mark Weidman. Mr. Weidman, you were describing the gymnasium lyceum which was a Jewish school. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Would you elaborate a little bit on what the school was like, the classes and teachers, et cetera?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember exactly the number of the class. It was a co-ed, girls and boys in the same class. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would say roughly about 30 total. 30, 35. Maybe between 25 and 35. I can't give you the exact-- the exact number. As a matter of fact, I have a-- one picture saved from the classroom which I'll show you later. And the education level was very high, very demanding. And I-- I was really considering myself a top student in my-- my class. And as a matter of fact, in the later years just before the graduation, I used to get the whole group of boys and girls coming to my house, I should tutor them and prepare them for the exam. At that time, I was living-- when it was already during the Russian occupation-- I was living by my uncle. My father couldn't afford to-- to have private quarters, to pay. And my uncle was always kidding me. Here the girls are coming already to see you. He called them the-- the-- the little pigeons. He nicknamed them, pigeons. And they're coming. And there was like a-- like a-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] a little house outdoors in the orchard. There, we were meeting. And I was-- and I was teaching them. And the same time, was preparing myself also for the exam. And I had friends, and I had a pretty good social life, I would say, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] for my age. There was no time for play. With the time was really occupied with studies. Were-- when I came home from school, I had to prepare already, had to-- just had my meal, and prepare for the-- for the next day, for-- do my homework. It was quite-- we were pretty-- I was pretty much occupied with studies. And they benefited really for me. I still remember a lot of-- a lot of-- of-- of subjects that I took up those-- at those days, particularly Latin and physics, math, and so forth. Sciences. And we-- we were very well prepared for-- for further education.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just one question about how did you spend the Shabbat. By yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, usually I was-- I was staying with a family, with a Jewish family. And I was-- they were-- they were inviting me, of course, for their sabbath meals. And they were-- they were cooking for me, and-- and I was participating together. But it was not the same like at home. It was different. The-- the family atmosphere was really missing, and I was lonely as far as that concerned. I miss my home. And I was very glad to come for the holidays and for vacations to my house, and refresh my-- my acquaintances and see my family, stay with my family. We were very happy-- happy days for me to come home for vacation or for the holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, the political scene was changing, of course. And did you have any idea of what was-- what changes were to come in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Poland was a pretty anti-Semitic country. The Jews never felt welcome in Poland. I could never acquire a-- a true friend, a Polish friend, or even less a Ukrainian friend. So my social life was really limited to-- to having Jewish friends, whether male or female. And being that my-- my school was Jewish. So those days, I didn't have much contact with the-- with the outside population. I was limited to my friends, my circle of friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any idea of what-- what was going on in Europe, and did you have any idea that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I saw--

speaker: interviewer
text: --life would change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was very well aware of the clouds that were coming down on Europe. I still remember when Hitler came to power in 1933, I was only 10 years old. But was the first signal, first bad omen for the Jewish people. And-- but somehow, we were optimistic that he'll never reach us. He'll never come to Poland. He will stay in Germany. He will be limited to Germany. But this-- but it was not to be. If not for that optimism-- false optimism, I would say-- a lot of Jewish people would have taken a different route, either would emigrate either to Palestine or to-- or to overseas. I had a possibility to go-- to go overseas to America. Because my father had a-- had a sister and a brother living in America. They emigrated before the First World War. But we never thought of it, that there will be a necessity to move and-- and break up our homes. This was the-- the false optimism that guided not only-- only me, but I would say the majority of the Jewish people in Eastern Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did life begin to change for you, the good life that you have described?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- September 1, 1939, the first German planes appeared over our town. That was the signal that the war started. I remember I was near panic. It was so unexpected, so sudden. And this was like my whole life just disappeared all of a sudden. The life I was accustomed to stopped-- cease-- stopped existing in that moment. And we-- I remember there was a few-- a few days, maybe 10 days, and the war was going on. When Poland was still there. And it was a panic. The Polish army was in a disarray. Being that we were not far from the Hungarian border, they did-- whole-- whole brigades, battalions, were slipping through the border escaping to Hungary. And we could have had-- we could have done the same thing at the time. But we never predicted there will be a necessity for us to do it. And it was much harder to break up a family which had its roots there. And we were waiting, what's-- what's-- what's going to come.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you personally see Polish soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- going through your town, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you describe this?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were in disarray already. They-- they dropped their weapons and that were their uniforms, and they're slipping through the mountains to-- to-- to Hungary. And from there, some of them stayed in Hungary. Some of them later, I found out, went to Turkey, through the Middle East. And there was-- there was probably-- some of them were wounded. And the war lasted a short time. I would say a few weeks. And about towards the end of September, I remember-- I would say around the 20th September, the Germans came. The Germans came to us. And it was a panic among the Jewish people. But they didn't manage to do any harm to us. But I remember it was Yom Kippur from 1939. We went out in synagogue. We were in a panic. We-- we-- we saw the German soldiers marching through the streets of Schodnica. I was in Schodnica at the time. I went home for the holidays. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the neighboring village, they have-- they managed to-- to the-- they gathered a-- a group of Polish soldiers and they shot them in cold blood in a-- in a church. In a neighboring village in [POLISH]. And we-- we were in a panic, what is going to-- we were-- we were-- we were worried we may be-- be next. But lo and behold, the next morning Russian tanks came into town. Apparently the Ribbentrop-Molotov agreement has settled that our town will be part of the Soviet Union. And sure enough, the Germans were-- were gone, were disappeared, and the Soviet army, the Red Army, came. We really looked at that time to the Red Army as liberators. Considering the-- the Nazis, the Germans, they were-- we-- we saw liberate-- liberators in them. But it was not really liberation because the Soviet period was not a happy period.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, hunger set in momentarily. I remember-- I remember within a week, there were bread lines. Everything was taken away. You have some possessions, worthy possessions were taken away from you. The oil field that belonged to my family were nationalized, were taken away. And the family became a paupers overnight. But they retained my father, being that he was a big expert in the field. They retained him as an employee in the oil fields. Where I-- I don't remember what the actual salary was, but it was pretty minimal. And we-- you couldn't survive on that salary. But life has changed drastically to us. We-- we became like paupers overnight. We had to depend on-- on the handouts of the Russian government.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me backtrack for a second about something you just said. You said that the Russians took over your oil fields. Did your family own oil fields?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, my family owned oil fields. It was a-- my family was pretty wealthy, and owned the oil fields. Because they owned the field before oil was discovered. And then oil was discovered in those fields. And then they became wealthy. It was a wealthy family in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now--

speaker: interviewee
text: Big, too. And in Schodnica, I would say total population of Schodnica, of-- of Jews, was about 600 people, approximately, Jews. I would say out of that, about two thirds was my family. Uncles, great uncles, cousins, what not. They were part of-- they were population of Schodnica.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the name of the family was Backenroth. That was a known family in all of Poland, of Galicia, especially. The name Backenroth was very known. It was synonymous with-- with wealth, with charity, and with [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] education, and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you said all this ended suddenly with the Russians taking over--

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the area. Could you describe how-- how this affected your immediate family. Your father, your mother, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Grandparents.

speaker: interviewee
text: it-- it affected us morally and materially. Morally, because we became like paupers overnight. You know? Everything was taken away from us. It was nationalized. Including-- including the fields and everything else. We-- we had a right to work in the fields, but it didn't belong to us anymore. Also there was a shortage of everything, include-- food in particular. Bread lines. I remember I used to get up at 4 o'clock in the morning to stay in bread lines to get a loaf of bread, and usually by the time I got to the end of the line, the-- the bread was gone already. There was no more bread. It was pretty-- pretty bad times. With starvation, with hunger. And did-- they were not happy memories for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did this last, the Russian--

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this lasted for--

speaker: interviewer
text: --occupation.

speaker: interviewee
text: --from September, 1939 till the-- the outbreak of the Russo-German war was in June, 1941. I remember it was a Sunday morning when the first German Luftwaffe planes were bombarding already. I was in Drohobycz at the time. They were bombarding the oil field-- the oil refineries. They were very important. And they wanted to destroy them. And the-- and this was a terrible, terrible experience to me. I might to-- I might want to mention that when I was accepted in medical school in Lwow, there was a-- a few days before the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took the last train from Lwow to Drohobycz [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] before the outbreak of the war, because I saw a lot of-- a lot of border police in-- in Lwow, with green-- with green caps on their heads. And I saw a bad omen in it. And I decided to leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came home to my uncle, to Drohobycz. It was a Saturday night. And Sunday morning, the war broke out. And there was a few days that the Russians-- a few days after the outbreak of the war, the Russians gave up. They ran away. The Russian army ran away back east, and there were a few days we were left. There were no-- no Germans yet, and no Russians. At that time, I was back in Schodnica. I went back home. We had nothing to do with Drohobycz anymore. Matter of fact, my uncle Moses, with his-- with his son came back-- went back with me to Schodnica, hoping that Schodnica is a safe place to hide in case-- in case there's trouble. Because they had trusted the Ukrainian population there. With they-- they had close relationship with the Jewish people at the time. Little did they know what's going to happen. When I came back to Schodnica at the time and I looked at those Ukrainian faces, I predicted that something is going to happen. And I-- we had a little, like a cellar outside, next to the barn. That cellar was built like this. Was built-- it had concrete walls, then covered with sod. And there-- and around the sod with a wooden structure. So I-- a few days after-- after-- before the Germans came, I made like a-- a hideaway there, a hiding place, in that cellar. Above-- in the attic.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember my-- my uncle was very optimistic. He said, what do you need this-- this hideaway place? Because what's going to happen to all the Jews will happen to you. There's no-- there's no reason to hide. But I didn't pay attention. And I made that hiding-- hiding place.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember it was a Thursday morning. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother-- it was 6 o'clock in the morning. My mother woke me up. She said she hears some shooting. So I got-- I got dressed quickly. And I remember my father had-- was in a night shift. He was not home. He was a night shift in the oil fields. And my uncle was standing with his-- with his prayer shawl, with his phylacteries and his-- on his head and his arm, and praying in the dining room. And my cousin was sleeping on the couch. So I-- I begged my uncle he should go with me to the-- to the hideaway. He-- he-- he would have none of it. But my cousin-- he was sleeping. I woke him up. And I didn't ask him any questions. And I pulled him along with me. And we ran in. And about 20-- and-- and we were in that attic hiding there. About 20 minutes later, armed Ukrainian bands came. They dragged out my uncle. I saw it through the-- between the boards of the attic. You could-- you could look through. And they were leading him out. In that day, those Ukrainian bands from Schodnica, from the surrounding villages, they had rifles somehow. They saved rifles or they stole rifles from the-- from the Soviet army. I don't know where the rifles came from. And they shot in the woods, surrounding woods in the mountains. About, I would say, 75% to 80% of the male population, they shot them. And the next day, those Ukrainians came for the women, they should bury them. And my mother and my sister, I remember, had to go and bury those-- those victims of that pogrom. And I was in this hideaway place maybe three or four days till things simmer down. Then the Germans came. That's when I-- we came out from the hideaway place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you and your cousin hid together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me try and clarify this. You mentioned there was a-- a little building next to the barn.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. A cellar.

speaker: interviewer
text: A cellar.

speaker: interviewee
text: We call the cellar. But what it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you said you hid in the attic.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a difference? Am I-- were you in-- in the cellar, or were you in the attic?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- that-- see, by-- by the entrance of the cellar was a little-- little like an entrance way. And over the-- the entrance way was a little attic. That's where we hid-- that's where we were hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what occurred to your father? He was not there. He was on the night shift.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My father was at night shift. And he-- he-- he ran to the woods. He-- he hid in the woods for a few days. As a matter of fact, about two or-- two days later, he came home. And my mother directed him to our hideaway. He came with a-- with a cousin of ours. Another cousin. And we stayed another two, three days with us over there. And my-- and at night my mother was reaching over some food, some-- to us, we should survive over there in that attic. I remember the day of the pogrom was a very hot day. And we had no water there, nothing. So I remember at night, I-- I-- I slipped out of the hideaway to the garden, because it was next to the garden. And I was licking the dew from the-- from the vegetable gardens to-- to quench my thirst. And I-- very quietly to make sure that no neighbors, no-- nobody hears me or sees me. It was-- it was only at night, I was doing it. But later-- later already, a couple of days later, my mother was at night reaching over some-- some drinks and some food to us into the hideaway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you said substantial portion of the male Jewish population--

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: --was killed by the Ukrainians.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there members of your family-- I assume your uncle.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There were-- there were-- I would say-- I would say at that time, uncles, great uncles, cousins. A lot of them were murdered in cold blood and buried in those woods surrounding Schodnica. They are in unmarked graves, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what happened then? After the Ukrainian pogrom, the Germans came in, you said.

speaker: interviewee
text: A few weeks after the Ukrainian pogrom, they declared-- the Germans were already entrenched. And they-- they declared Schodnica Judenrein. Which means the whole Jewish population had to evacuate, whoever was still alive, and go either to Borislav, which is next town, or to the following towns. Drohobycz. And there was still another town, Sambor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the survivors of that pogrom were-- were-- they-- they spread out on those three places. Borislav, Drohobycz, and Sambor. We chose to go to Drohobycz, being that we had a-- an uncle that was killed already. But he had a big house in Drohobycz. And we went there, and we stayed in their house in Drohobycz. Could you tell me who-- what members of your family you are including?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, my father, my mother, my sister, and my grandmother. But we couldn't accommodate my grandmother because she lived with us all the time. She went to-- to live with an aunt of ours, that she lived also in Drohobycz. Her name was Pearl Reich. And she lived with her, and she stayed there with her till she was killed by the Nazis in her own bed at the age of 93.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather passed away in the late '30s before the war started. My grandfather-- paternal grandfather. My maternal grandparents, they were killed in the extermination camp in Belzec in 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let us continue with your history. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You are now in Drohobycz, in the house of your uncle. And what happened then to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Then-- then-- I had a cousin of ours. His name was-- he was an engineer. He was an agronomic engineer. His name was-- his name, Naftali Backenroth. Later after the war changed to Nicholas Bronicki. He died in Israel a couple of years ago. He organized a group of young Jewish people to-- to make a work brigade, to work for the gestapo. To make them feel that the Jews are useful people, and they can produce, make things-- things worthwhile. This way, we should be able to survive. He hoped we were able to survive, they will spare us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and I belonged-- I joined that group. And we-- we worked-- they called the group of the Schupo. Schupo is an acronym for [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And we worked for the gestapo. What did we do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We performed all kinds of work. They had a-- they were very keen of horse riding. And they had a stable of horses. So some people worked in the horse stable. Also they-- they started a project to build a so-called [GERMAN], a-- a-- a horse riding academy. And we built it. At that time, also we-- they-- they employed also [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Polish and Ukrainian masons. Of course, they were paid for it. And we were the slave labor. And at that time, I decided to learn masonry. And I attached myself to a group of Gentile masons, and-- as a helper. And they liked me, sort of. And they taught me masonry. And it-- as a matter of fact, they accepted me to the official Masonic-- Masonic organization. Then they declared me an official mason. I mastered the trade. And that came in very handy to me in my later years during the Nazi occupation. And I was committing-- doing that-- the slave work there till about the fall of 1942. During that period of time, there were a lot of [GERMAN], so-called, and the-- they decimated the Jewish population very substantially. And they-- they were deported to the extermination camp, basically to Belzec. We got wind of it. We found out where they going. And-- but our group was somehow-- because of our productivity, we were spared and they did not deport us. But it was about October-- end of October of 1942, my cousin, that-- I before mentioned, engineer Naftali Backenroth, approached me. And he says, you know, the outlook here is not very good for Jewish people. I have a lot of confidence in you. He meant me. I know somebody that came back from Germany [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] for vacation, for the winter vacation. He works in Germany. And he has a permit to cross the Polish-German border because he has to go back-- back to work there, after the holidays. I could arrange it-- of course, it'll cost some money-- to buy this permit from him, to use it just for-- for the crossing of the German-Polish border, go to Germany. And I have confidence in you that you will manage to pull this one and survive the war. If you're willing to do it, let me know. I went back home. I remember at that time, my mother was hiding in the bunker, because of course the [GERMAN] was taking place. And I went to the bunker. And I remember her pale face. And I told her what was offered to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she started crying and encouraging me, you must do it. You must do it. Because we will-- we will perish here. Try to survive. Somebody should survive from the family to be able to tell what happened here.
